text: The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to mobilize the colon from the splenic flexure, starting at the white line. The colon is separated from the perinephric fat, with careful attention to the color of the fat, and mobilized anteriorly down to the level of the iliac vessels. The colonic mesentery is reflected at the level of the kidney's upper pole.

The surgeon uses traction and electrocautery to dissect at the level of the pedicle, separating the colonic mesentery from the perinephric fat. The colon is fully reflected, revealing the renal vein. Dissection continues to identify the ureter and gonadal complex, with the opened Gerota's fascia visible.

The surgeon observes visible peristalsis in the ureter while lifting the ureter and gonadal complex, creating a window anterior to the psoas sheath. The lower pole of the kidney is elevated, revealing the renal vein and a pulsating artery, likely the lower pole renal artery, which is clipped, ligated, and divided. Using suction, the dissection continues towards the pedicle, focusing on the renal vein where the gonadal vein drains into it. Posterior to the renal vein, the renal artery is identified, and sharp dissection is performed around it.

The surgeon clears the lymphatic tissue and sheath around the renal artery, applying double clips on the patient side and one clip on the specimen side. The renal artery is divided, and the renal vein is clipped and divided, leaving a satisfactory stump length. The dissection continues, encountering and clipping the lumbar vein, which is then ligated and divided. Further dissection proceeds posterior to the upper pole of the kidney.

The surgeon identifies the area of the tumor at the upper pole of the kidney, where several tumor vessels are visible and ligated before being divided. Dissection proceeds to separate the Lenorenal ligament from the kidney using electrocautery, revealing the surface of the tumor.

The surgeon ligates and divides the ureter, then clears the remaining attachments of the specimen from the swash sheath and lateral abdominal wall. The specimen is retrieved through a small lower abdominal incision, revealing a clear renal fossa.

and patient was allowed diet on post-op day one. Follies removed on day one and drain was removed on day two. Patient discharged on day three.
